questions.prompt: Your support bot doesn't know your product's pricing or feature details. A teammate suggests fine-tuning on your docs to fix it. Good plan?

choices.text: Yes — fine-tuning is how you teach a model facts.

No. Fine-tuning is for behavior, tone, and format — not for teaching facts. The bot will memorize the docs fuzzily and still hallucinate. Use RAG: retrieve relevant doc chunks at query time and pass them as context. Hallucinations drop, and updating the docs doesn't require retraining.
Yes, but only if you fine-tune on GPT-5.

questions.explanation: Fine-tuning shapes how the model talks, not what it knows. Facts learned in fine-tuning are unreliable — the model treats them statistically rather than as ground truth. RAG is the right tool for fact lookup: retrieve, then condition the model on retrieved text.

questions.prompt: You have a 3,000-token system prompt enforcing a specific brand voice. Outputs are mostly right but drift, and the long prompt makes every call slow and expensive. What's the natural fit for fine-tuning?

choices.text: Not a fit — fine-tuning won't help with voice.

Strong fit. Collect 200–500 examples of on-voice copy, fine-tune a mini-tier model on them. The resulting model 'knows' your voice from training, so you can use a much shorter inference-time prompt. Cost drops, latency drops, consistency improves.
Switch to GPT-5 with the same long prompt.
Train a new model from scratch.

questions.explanation: This is the canonical fine-tuning use case: narrow stylistic behavior the base model can do but doesn't do consistently. Fine-tuning compresses the long system prompt into the model weights, so inference-time prompts get shorter and outputs more consistent. Best paired with a mini-tier base for cost savings.
